response: The EU’s General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), effective May 2018, applies globally to any organization handling EU residents’ personal data and grants rights such as access, erasure, portability, and explicit consent, while mandating Data Protection Officers for large‑scale processing and 72‑hour breach notifications. Violations can incur fines up to 4 % of global annual revenue or €20 million (whichever is higher), exemplified by Meta’s €1.2 billion fine in 2023, Amazon’s €746 million penalty, and Google’s cumulative fines exceeding €200 million. GDPR has spurred comparable privacy laws worldwide (e.g., California’s CCPA, Brazil’s LGPD, India’s DPDPA) but faces criticism for imposing heavy compliance costs on small firms, generating user fatigue with cookie banners, and showing uneven enforcement across member states. Nonetheless, it has cemented data privacy as a fundamental right and set the global benchmark for data protection legislation.
